content.problemStatement:
You are given a **2D matrix** matmatmat of size m×nm \times nm×n consisting of positive integers. An element mat[i][j]mat[i][j]mat[i][j] is called a **peak element** if it is strictly greater than all of its adjacent neighbours (up, down, left, right).

You need to find **a peak element** in the matrix.

If multiple peak elements exist, **return any one of them**.

Output the position of the peak as [i,j][i, j][i,j] (0-indexed).

You can assume that the matrix is surrounded by an outer perimeter of −1-1−1 (for comparison), and no two adjacent cells are equal.

Your solution must run in O(mlog⁡n)O(m \log n)O(mlogn) or O(nlog⁡m)O(n \log m)O(nlogm) time.

Function DeclarationFunction NamefindPeakGridfindPeakGridfindPeakGrid – This function finds the position of a peak element in a 2D grid.

Parameters
mmm : An integer representing the number of rows in the matrix.
nnn : An integer representing the number of columns in the matrix.
matmatmat : A 2D array/matrix of integers.
Return Value
Returns a list/array/pair containing exactly two integers [i,j][i, j][i,j], where iii is the row index and jjj is the column index of the peak element.
Constraints
1≤m,n≤5001 \le m, n \le 5001≤m,n≤500
1≤mat[i][j]≤2.5×1051 \le mat[i][j] \le 2.5 \times 10^51≤mat[i][j]≤2.5×105
No two adjacent cells are equal
The matrix is conceptually surrounded by −1-1−1
At least one peak element always exists
Time complexity must be O(mlog⁡n)O(m \log n)O(mlogn) or O(nlog⁡m)O(n \log m)O(nlogm)

Input Format

The first line contains two space-separated integers mmm and nnn — the number of rows and columns.
The next mmm lines each contain nnn space-separated integers representing the matrix elements.


Output Format
Print two **space-separated integers** iii and jjj — the position of the **peak**.
constraints: No two adjacent cells are equal
The matrix is conceptually surrounded by -1
At least one peak element always exists
content.inputFormat: The first line contains two space-separated integers m and n — the number of rows and columns. The next m lines each contain n space-separated integers representing the matrix elements.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print two space-separated integers i and j (0-indexed) denoting the position of any peak element.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Column binary search picks column 1, finds max row 0 (value 4). Both neighbours are smaller, so (0,1) is a peak.
Mid column 1, max row 1 (value 30). Left neighbour 21 and right neighbour 14 are smaller, also up/down are smaller, thus (1,1) is a peak.
Binary search eventually examines column 2, where the maximum is 32 at row 2. Its neighbours (16 left, -1 right, 14 up, 9 down) are all smaller, so (2,2) is a peak.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using a binary search approach. We start by finding the middle column and then finding the maximum element in that column. If the maximum element is greater than its neighbors, we have found a peak element. Otherwise, we move to the left or right half of the matrix based on the comparison of the maximum element with its left and right neighbors.
solution.timeComplexity: O(m log n) per test case
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — no extra space is used.
